retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Treatment / Management -- When do we restore?
retrieved_docs.content: “Noncleansable cavitated dentine caries cannot be managed by biofilm removal, remineralization, or sealing alone. However, in the primary dentition, these lesions might be transformable into cleansable lesions and managed via nonrestorative cavity control.” [2] “Certain occlusal lesions might appear clinically non-cavitated but radiographically extend significantly into dentine. If such lesions cannot be arrested through biofilm control alone, fissure sealing can be carried out; however, the integrity of the sealant needs to be monitored, and there is a possibility, until more evidence has emerged, that a “trampoline” effect may lead to failure of the sealant and restoration will be required.” [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Dental sealants are recommended for both permanent and primary teeth. In permanent molars, resin sealants have been shown to reduce caries incidence for up to 4 years compared to no treatment. [10] The evidence on sealing cavitated caries is mixed; while some studies report success in arresting micro-cavitated lesions, others show limited efficacy beyond noncavitated stages. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Extensive evidence supports sealants as an effective intervention for both preventing caries and arresting noncavitated lesions. One study in children demonstrated a 37% reduction in caries risk with pit and fissure sealants compared to a control group. Another study found that, over a 3-year period, first permanent molars treated with sealants showed a 44% lower risk of developing caries compared to untreated teeth. [3] Caries risk assessment remains essential in guiding clinical decisions, and each patient’s risk status should be reassessed periodically to optimize preventive care.

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Technique or Treatment -- Evaluation of the Sealant and Occlusion of Sealed Tooth Surfaces
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, the occlusion should be checked with articulating paper, and any interference should be adjusted using a round finishing bur. Annual recalls should be conducted, as 5% to 10% of sealants typically require repair or replacement each year. Consistent follow-up ensures long-term effectiveness and prevents complications related to caries development.

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Technique or Treatment -- Follow-up
retrieved_docs.content: Sealed surfaces should be regularly monitored clinically. Bitewing radiographs are recommended for radiographic assessment, which should be performed as often as the risk status indicates. However, the risk status may change over time; therefore, other susceptible sites, eg, proximal surfaces, should also be monitored. Defective sealants must be reapplied to maintain marginal integrity. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Clinical Significance -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries prognosis depends on the patient's health, maintenance of oral hygiene, and the extent and severity of the lesion. Ideally, full recovery of an initial caries lesion would be achieved with the described non-operative measures and are preferred. Despite this, a minimal intervention therapy can also be used, like pit and fissure sealants using resin-based or glass ionomer agents). Dentists should only consider restoring if the initial lesion progresses. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- History and Physical -- Recurrent Caries
retrieved_docs.content: A recurrent or secondary caries is a new caries that form at the restoration’s margin. [17] A microleakage of the restoration causes it; however, this leakage does not result in an active demineralization under the filling. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Pit and fissure sealants represent an evidence-based strategy for preventing dental caries, particularly in children and individuals with limited access to care. The effectiveness of this intervention relies not only on proper material selection and clinical technique but also on early identification of at-risk patients and ongoing maintenance. High-risk populations benefit most when multiple healthcare professionals contribute to prevention efforts through coordinated roles and responsibilities.

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The use of sealants should be guided by individual caries risk, not just eruption status. Although no diagnostic tool perfectly predicts caries development, a history of caries remains the most reliable indicator of risk for caries development. Resin-based sealants are generally more effective than glass ionomer cements due to superior sealing ability. Nevertheless, glass ionomer materials still have a role, especially in situations where rubber dam isolation is not possible. Even when dentin is involved, sealing can be effective if a tight bond is achieved. In primary teeth, sealants may perform comparably well as more invasive procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Dental professionals should determine the need for a pit and fissure sealant based on the patient’s individual risk rather than age or the time since tooth eruption. [15] For patients without risk factors or those at low risk for developing carious lesions, applying a sealant may not be necessary at that time. Regular monitoring remains essential for all children to identify any changes in cariogenic risk factors, as well as clinical or radiographic signs of dental decay. [9] Ongoing assessment ensures timely intervention when risk levels increase, allowing preventive measures to be implemented effectively and maintaining optimal oral health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Function -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pit and fissure sealant: GIC-based fissure sealants offer less retention than resin-based ones, only indicated as temporary sealants in newly erupted permanent teeth. The caries preventive action of GIC-based pit and fissure sealants is comparable to resin-based pit and fissure sealants. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Treatment / Management -- Newer Treatment Approach
retrieved_docs.content: The traditional restorative and surgical way of managing early childhood caries requires general anesthesia with its attendant health risks and is expensive and does not prevent further caries but only treats the result of the disease. Therefore, up to 79% of patients treated with conventional therapy relapse. [32] This is why the new management of ECC focuses on preventive measures, including parental involvement, preventive programs with active monitoring of the condition, temporary restorations, and postpones advanced restorations.

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pit and fissure sealants act as a protective physical barrier, preventing the accumulation of food particles and microorganisms. Their use has proven effective in preventing the initiation of caries and arresting early noncavitated lesions. [3] [4] Among all teeth, permanent first molars exhibit the highest prevalence of caries, followed by second molars. These teeth are especially vulnerable because caries often begin shortly after eruption, and they are the first permanent molars to erupt. The long-term effectiveness of sealants relies heavily on their retention and correct application. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Treatment / Management -- Non-invasive Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: We do not need to place a filling in a lesion that can be accessed by easy cleaning measures that will disturb the biofilm, such as fluoride-containing toothpaste. [1] The following lesions can be managed conservatively without the need for a filling: White spot lesions, including initial occlusal lesions. [1] [19] When on a bitewing x-ray, approximal lesions are limited to the enamel or are just into dentin. (It is improbable that these lesions are cavitated in modern populations). [1] Cavitated and non-cavitated root surface lesions can be reached by cleaning implements. [20] [1] If a recurrent lesion adjacent to a restoration is cleansable, it does not require restoration. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Sealants can be applied to occlusal surfaces, as well as to smooth and proximal surfaces. Research supports their use in preventing both occlusal surface caries and arresting noncavitated and microcavitated lesions on proximal surfaces. [11] [13] [14] Sealants may be applied to both fully and partially erupted molars. However, retention is lower in the latter due to moisture control challenges, making glass ionomer a preferable choice in such cases. They are also effective on fluorotic teeth, with resin sealants showing significantly better retention than glass ionomer sealants after 1 year. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pit and fissure sealant Cementation of post and core Restoration of deciduous teeth Class III and V lesions

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2820
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم, a condition in which the uterine cervix is unable to maintain sufficient closure during pregnancy, leading to an increased risk of preterm birth or pregnancy loss. The cerclage technique involves placing a suturing material around the cervix to reinforce its structural integrity, thereby preventing premature cervical dilation. This intervention is employed as a therapeutic measure to manage cervical insufficiency and improve obstetric outcomes for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of cerclage from cervix is a medical intervention that is directly related to the cervical cerclage procedure (إجراء تطويق عنق الرحم). This step involves extracting the sutures that were placed around the cervix to reinforce cervical competence and prevent premature dilation, and it represents a specific phase within the overall management of cervical insufficiency. Recognizing the connection between the removal of the cerclage and the broader cerclage procedure is essential for appropriate patient care and timing of intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2707
retrieved_docs.content: خديج is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم), indicating a direct link between this condition and weakened cervical competence. Additionally, recurrent miscarriage (إجهاض معتاد) is identified as a parent condition of cervical insufficiency, suggesting that women who experience repeated pregnancy loss may have an underlying cervical insufficiency contributing to the miscarriages. Recognizing both the association of خديج with cervical insufficiency and the role of إجهاض معتاد as a precursor condition is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of patients at risk for cervical incompetence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN” is clinically associated with “ولادة قبل الأوان” (preterm birth). This relationship suggests that when a caesarean section scar cannot be classified, there may be an increased likelihood of delivering before term, highlighting the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management in patients with indeterminate scar types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2497
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم) is a condition in which the uterine cervix is unable to maintain closure during pregnancy, often leading to second‑trimester loss. Recurrent miscarriage (إجهاض معتاد) is identified as a parent or underlying factor linked to cervical insufficiency, underscoring the need to assess cervical competence in patients with habitual abortion. The cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency as a therapeutic intervention that reinforces the cervix to prevent premature dilation and improve pregnancy outcomes. Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is also clinically associated with cervical insufficiency, indicating that cervical competence may influence the decision‑making and management of planned pregnancy termination. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose, counsel, and manage women at risk for cervical insufficiency and its related obstetric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2769
retrieved_docs.content: Low transverse cesarean section is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), indicating that this surgical approach to delivery may be linked to an increased occurrence of early membrane rupture, a factor that clinicians must consider when planning obstetric care and managing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy and تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) are both classified under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد (obstetric therapeutic procedures). By recognizing these interventions as subcomponents of the broader category of obstetric therapeutic care, clinicians can better organize treatment options, understand their related indications, and apply appropriate management strategies within the field of obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2007
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy observable is employed as an interpretive tool for إجهاض معتاد, indicating that observable signs and findings during pregnancy can be used to recognize or assess the condition of recurrent miscarriage. This relationship underscores the clinical importance of monitoring pregnancy‑related observations to identify and manage habitual abortion effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth is recognized as a specific manifestation of a previous c-section, indicating that a prior cesarean delivery can create complications in subsequent pregnancy or childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1447
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) and REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR are both clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم. قصور عنق الرحم, also known as cervical insufficiency, is a condition in which the cervix is unable to maintain its structural integrity during pregnancy, increasing the risk of premature dilation and preterm delivery. The cerclage procedure involves placing a suturing stitch around the cervix to reinforce it and prevent premature opening, thereby directly addressing the mechanical weakness characteristic of قصور عنق الرحم. Additionally, a poor reproductive history—such as prior miscarriages, preterm births, or other adverse pregnancy outcomes—has been linked to a higher likelihood of developing قصور عنق الرحم, indicating that past obstetric complications can serve as a clinical indicator for this cervical disorder. Recognizing both the therapeutic role of cerclage and the predictive value of reproductive history is essential for early identification and management of patients at risk for قصور عنق الرحم.
